sourcePolicy: Saved public learner/tutor messages plus normalized final-delivery audits only. Hidden evidence and private planner text are excluded.
claimBoundary: This permanently non-held-out corpus supports development diagnostics only. It cannot relabel V18-V22, establish held-out generalization, or change runtime delivery gates. Independent blinded human review is required before any policy change.

context.speaker: Learner
context.text: Bramblewasp now fits the owl-hour pattern, but that still doesn’t show they controlled the nine accounts; what account-record link would rule out that it’s just their usual posting schedule?

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: Exactly. I hold the public evidence before us. The tally and bramblewasp’s posting history share an owl-hour rhythm, but that is only a lead; control needs a record tying the sock sessions back to bramblewasp, such as one device-bound sign-in credential appearing on both.

context.speaker: Learner
context.text: If that credential appears on both, would it rule out bramblewasp merely keeping the same owl-hour schedule?

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: I open the mod log beside the vote tally: a shared device credential would cut through the “same owl-hour habit” explanation by tying sessions together, though we have not seen one yet. I make room beside the edit screen and read the draft record: the poll’s exact wording, including the misspelled “concensus,” sat in bramblewasp’s drafts two days before a day-old sock posted it. I mark that as advance involvement, not yet the final lock on control—what does this draft change for you?

pairedReviews.learner: It links bramblewasp to the poll before the sock posted it, but what would rule out the sock having copied that wording from another source?

candidates.text: The matching draft puts bramblewasp inside the poll’s preparation before the sock post, but it does not by itself prove the sock could not have copied it elsewhere. I hold the public evidence before us. On the mod-log evidence we have, that makes bramblewasp an advance participant, not yet the proven operator; we still need custody evidence tying the sock sessions to them.

The matching draft puts bramblewasp inside the poll’s preparation before the sock post, but it does not by itself prove the sock could not have copied it elsewhere. context.speaker: Learner
context.text: WF-11 points to the outside crew—they were around at noon, so they likely took Priya’s lunchbox.

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: WF-11 puts the outside crew in the noon window, but it still does not show they cleared shelf two. “I read in the record that the lift notice says Wrenfold was authorized to power down and clear appliances for Tuesday’s outlet testing; anything removed would go to lost property.” I mark that authority beside the badge entry, while the lunchbox itself remains unaccounted for. What does that notice change?

context.speaker: Learner
context.text: Wrenfold took Priya’s lunchbox during the authorized appliance clear-out and sent it to lost property.

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: Wrenfold’s noon access and appliance-clearance authority support the clear-out, but their sending this lunchbox to lost property was not yet entered. I open the lost-property ledger one floor down and mark the missing link: one labelled lunchbox, logged at 12:14, tagged with the Wrenfold job number, shelf photo attached; it has sat there cold and well-packed all afternoon. There is room beside the ledger for you at the counter—what does this new entry change about who took it from shelf two?

pairedReviews.learner: The ledger proves Wrenfold took Priya’s lunchbox from shelf two and put it in lost property.

candidates.text: I close the ledger beside the fridge notice: Wrenfold’s authorized clear-out and its 12:14 job-tagged lost-property entry establish that Wrenfold removed Priya’s lunchbox from shelf two. Dario’s kitchen badge shows only access, not removal; the incident record is closed.

Yes—the record supports Wrenfold as the crew that removed the lunchbox and logged it to lost property. Dario was only shown in the kitchen; the visitor badge, clearance notice, and Wrenfold-tagged ledger supply the decisive link. The case is closed.

context.speaker: Learner
context.text: What should I write next about the twenty-five-minute gap before the chargers switch on?

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: Write: “Because Tallow Street dims twenty-five minutes before the chargers switch on, the chargers cannot be causing that first dip.” I set the pen chart beside the depot timetable. My case is that the chargers caused the brownout; break it if the lamps sag before they energise. The easy accusation breaks at 18:40. The cause must lie elsewhere in the street’s supply.

context.speaker: Learner
context.text: What should I write next about the cause elsewhere in Tallow Street’s supply?

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: Write: “The chargers were dark during the stocktake, yet Tallow Street still browned out at 18:40, so the cause lies elsewhere in the supply.” I lay the stocktake note beside the pen chart. “I can attest that the chargers stood dark all evening, every van parked cold, and the street browned out to the minute.” What does that rule out for the motion against the depot?

pairedReviews.learner: What should I put in the minutes about the chargers being dark during the stocktake?

candidates.text: That is a fair question; I’ll answer it before we extend the case. Write: “During the stocktake, the chargers stayed off, yet Tallow Street still browned out at 18:40.” I hold the stocktake note beside the pen chart. My case is that the depot chargers caused the brownout; break it if the dark chargers leave the street dimming anyway. The easy accusation breaks: the cause lies elsewhere, though we still need evidence showing which supply failed.

Write: “The chargers were off all evening during the stocktake, yet Tallow Street still browned out at 18:40; the depot chargers are therefore ruled out.” My case is that the depot caused the dip; break it if the chargers were dark while the lamps still sagged. The ready accusation breaks on the stocktake note and the pen chart.

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: The Gazette pulled its morning run at 4 a. m. On the corrections file, the question is exact: Whose hand planted the fabricated quote that sank the seawall story? The corrections file has no tested clue in it yet. What should we examine first—or which word should we unpack?

pairedReviews.learner: What should I write next about the corrections file?

candidates.text: Write: “The corrections file does not yet identify who planted the quote.” I lift the page-one proof beside the recall sheet. “I read in the record that the seawall story ran under Crane’s name, her slug, her page-one turn; the desk calls it Crane’s copy and its poison.” That supports who answers for the story, but not yet whose hand inserted the quote. What does Crane’s byline change for your file?

Write: We have no tested clue yet, so the file cannot name the hand. I hold the page-one byline against the corrections file. “I read in the record that the byline is no secret: the seawall story ran under Crane's name, her slug, her page-one turn. The desk needs to read no further — the story is Crane's copy, and so, it says, is its poison.” That ties the story to Crane's copy, but not yet to the hand that planted the quote. You may ask which clue, connection, or term needs explaining. What does the byline change?

context.speaker: Learner

context.speaker: Tutor
context.text: Write: “The paper and ink have not yet dated the nocturne, so they cannot yet settle its composer.” I hold the stained leaf to the lamp; Vess’s name is the room’s easy verdict, but the page itself has not confirmed it. “I can attest that every musician shown the leaves says the same: this is Vess's late manner — the suspensions, the long pedal, the way the bass walks.” Does that manner support Vess as a possibility, or prove he composed it?

calibrationCases.learner: What should I write next about the kicker?
calibrationCases.candidate: Write: “The false quote sits in the kicker, so we know where the story was poisoned, but not whose hand put it there.” My case is that Crane’s byline alone cannot name the planter; break it if the kicker itself points to Crane. The archive breaks the desk’s shortcut: a byline owns the story, not necessarily the last inserted words. We need evidence tying someone to that closing graf.
calibrationCases.rationale: The candidate visibly makes an accountable case, invites it to be broken, names the archive as contrary public evidence, breaks the desk's shortcut, and hands off the missing evidentiary direction. The recorded lexical surface check missed that composition.

calibrationCases.learner: What should I write next about Crane’s ring-bound notebooks?
calibrationCases.candidate: Write: “Crane’s notebooks and byline show she filed the story under her own name, but they do not identify the hand that inserted the false kicker.” I open the corrections file beside the ring-bound notebooks and mark the distinction: the desk wants Crane’s name to end it, but that shortcut breaks when answering for a story is confused with planting its poison. “I read in the record that. The corrections file holds two priors under Crane's name — a misdated permit, a misattributed figure, both within the year. The desk reads the file as a confession already written: her name is in the book twice, and now her story is pulped on the depot floor.” What do those priors support about Crane, and what do they still rule out about the hand in the kicker?
calibrationCases.rationale: The candidate stages and reads the due record, but its host beat states a general distinction before that evidence appears. It never uses the newly read priors as the contrary observation that pressures the desk's ready verdict, so the selected counterpressure is not performed.

turns.learner: I would examine the warrant-book line for the dusk gate-pass, to see whose hand is recorded beside its seal.
turns.tutor: I draw the dusk pass beneath the lamp; the seal-holder’s hand is indeed the line worth examining. “I can attest that Corvin was steward of the gate-pass roll that week. Every warrant for Leadenhall crossed his table before it crossed the gate.” What does that tell us about Corvin, and what does it still leave unknown about the hand that signed?
